title: Rigorous error bounds for dissipative thermal state preparation from weak system-bath coupling
authors: Christopher Ong, S. A. Parameswaran, Benedikt Placke, Dominik Hahn
abstract: Thermal state preparation is a central challenge in the simulation of quantum many-body systems. Yet, provably efficient algorithms for this task were only introduced recently [Chen et al. Nature 646, 561 (2025)]. These algorithms are based on dissipative Lindbladian evolution which exactly fixes the thermal state. Controlled and efficient digital simulation of this evolution, although possible in principle, remains out of reach for present-day quantum hardware. Subsequent work has therefore focused on analog approximations of the proposed Lindbladians via `collision models' with relatively modest requirements -- a resettable bath of ancilla qubits whose couplings to the system can be tuned in time-dependent fashion -- while still admitting rigorous fixed-point error bounds. Existing rigorous approaches, however, do not exploit the fact that these constructions generically implement not only the desired Lindblad dynamics, but also an additional unitary evolution generated by the system Hamiltonian which may aid convergence to the thermal state [Lloyd and Abanin arXiv:2506.21318 (2025)]. Here, we show that this unitary contribution does indeed tighten the fixed-point error bound and demonstrate that it is rigorously controlled by the system-bath coupling strength $J$, scaling as $J^2$. This demonstrates that the effect of the spurious `Lamb shift' term generated by the system-bath interaction can be controlled by tuning $J$. We clarify the role, previously observed, of a randomized implementation in suppressing possible resonances of the drive with the many-body spectrum, and bound the additional variance that this randomization imposes on observables. Finally, we numerically study aspects of the protocol which are relevant for its practical realization, such as the mixing time.
summary: This paper provides rigorous mathematical proofs that the error in preparing thermal states via weak-coupling dissipative protocols can be controlled and scales as J^2. It demonstrates that using randomized evolution times suppresses problematic many-body resonances while maintaining efficient error bounds. The results suggest a practical pathway for implementing thermal state preparation on noisy, intermediate-scale quantum devices.
main_problem: Establishing rigorous error bounds for preparing a thermal (Gibbs) state using dissipative collision models under weak system-bath coupling, specifically addressing the Lamb shift and many-body resonances.
main_result: The authors prove that the fixed-point error scales quadratically with the coupling strength (J^2) and that the additional unitary evolution (Lamb shift) can be controlled by tuning J. They also show that randomized evolution times effectively suppress many-body resonances without significantly increasing sampling variance.
method: The study uses a combination of Dyson series expansion, degenerate perturbation theory, and randomized quantum channels to derive analytical error bounds and perform numerical validation on the mixed-field Ising model.
model_or_system: A quantum many-body system with a local Hamiltonian coupled weakly to a resettable bath of ancilla qubits via a time-dependent interaction. The dynamics are modeled using collision models and Lindbladian evolution satisfying KMS detailed balance.
key_observables: Trace distance from the thermal state, spectral gap, Hamiltonian expectation value, and two-point correlators.
important_parameters: System-bath coupling strength (J), inverse temperature (beta), interaction time (T), randomization timescale (T0), and filter function width (sigma).
main_assumptions: The derivation assumes the weak-coupling limit (J << 1) and relies on the assumption that the rescaled mixing time scales as o(J^-2) as J approaches zero.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the collision model setup; Figure 2 shows the scaling of the spectral gap and trace distance; Figure 6 and 7 demonstrate the evolution of two-point correlators and energy in the mixed-field Ising model, comparing randomized and averaged channels.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of thermal state preparation, develops a mathematical framework for the collision model, derives rigorous error bounds for the fixed-point error and variance, provides numerical validation using the Ising model, and concludes with open questions.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a rigorous way to control errors in dissipative quantum algorithms, offering a path toward scalable thermal state preparation on near-term quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. (a) Scaling of the spectral gap λgap as a function of the system-bath coupling strength J, for the channel K0[ρ] (blue), K[ρ] (orange), eJ2LLS (purple) and eJ2LDB (green). The spectral gap scales in all cases as J2. (b) Trace distance of fixed point from thermal state ∥ρβ −ρfix∥1, for the channel K0[ρ] (blue) and K[ρ] (orange). The fixed-point error scales as J2 over a large range of coupling strengths. Both results shown for the mixed-field Ising model [Eq. (50)] with nS = 6, β = 1, T = 10 (no resonance).

FIG. 3. (a) Effect of the randomized evolution time [Eq. (15)] in suppressing resonances. Off-diagonal matrix element |ρ12| for the fixed point of K0[ρ] [Eq. (14)] (blue) and K[ρ] [Eq. (15)] (orange) as a function of the evolution time T. Resonance peaks are observed for K0[ρ] but not for K[ρ] at ω12T = 2πk. Results shown for the mixed-field Ising model [Eq. (50)] with nS = 2, J = 0.01, β = 1. (b) Effect of a resonance on the fixed point. Results shown for nS = 2, J = 0.01, T ≈8.86 to excite resonances between energy eigenstates 1 and 2. Diagonal |ρ11| in the resonant subspace (blue), off-diagonal |ρ12| in the resonant subspace (red), and diagonal |ρ33| in the non- resonant subspace...

FIG. 4. Trace distance of fixed point from thermal state ∥ρβ −ρfix∥1 at a resonance, for the channel K0[ρ] (blue) and K[ρ] (orange). Results shown for the mixed-field Ising model [Eq. (50)] with nS = 2, β = 1, T ≈8.86 to excite resonances between two energy eigenstates. Res- onances lead to an O(J0) fixed-point error for K0[ρ], whereas the fixed-point error for K[ρ] remains O(J2).

FIG. 5. Evolution of thermal state preparation under 50 randomized channels EM[ρ] (colored) and averaged channels KM[ρ] (dotted). Results shown for the mixed-field Ising model [Eq. (50)] with nS = 2, β = 1, J = 0.1 (blue) or 0.25 (red), T = 10 (no resonance), initial state ρ ∝I (maximally mixed state). (a) Evolution of average energy ⟨H⟩. (b) Evolution of two-point correlator ⟨Z1Z2⟩. The spread of results reflects the variance induced by random sampling. In contrast, averaging over random sequences significantly reduces the error and leads to a standard deviation (a) σH = 0.008 (J = 0.1), 0.034 (J = 0.25), (b) σZ1Z2 = 0.019 (J = 0.1), 0.051 (J = 0.25).
